data.title: Meta is said to recruit more AI talent from OpenAI
data.description:
Meta Platforms (NASDAQ:META) is continuing its hiring spree of artificial intelligence experts from OpenAI, with four more researchers set to join the company, according to a report from The Information on Saturday.

The new recruits -- Shengjia Zhao, Jiahui Yu, Shuchao Bi and Hongyu Ren -- have each agreed to join Meta, the report said, citing a source familiar with the matter.

This follows news earlier in the week that Meta (NASDAQ:META) had hired Lucas Beyer, Alexander Kolesnikov and Xiaohua Zhai, all of whom previously worked in OpenAI’s Zurich office, according to the Wall Street Journal.

Both Meta (META) and OpenAI, the developer behind ChatGPT, declined to comment when contacted by Reuters.

Meta (META) has recently accelerated its efforts to attract AI talent from OpenAI, as Chief Executive Mark Zuckerberg expands the company's work in advanced artificial intelligence and what's known as superintelligence research.

data.title: Elon Musk slams Senate tax and spending bill, warns of economic fallout
data.description:
Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA) Chief Executive Elon Musk sharply criticized the latest version of President Donald Trump's tax and spending proposal, calling it "utterly insane and destructive" just weeks after the two appeared to reconcile following earlier disagreements over the legislation.

Musk, who previously clashed with Trump over the bill's contents, took to social platform X on Saturday to voice his concerns. "This Senate bill will destroy millions of American jobs and inflict significant long-term harm on the country," Musk posted.

The billionaire entrepreneur argued that the proposed legislation favors outdated industries while undermining sectors critical to the future of U.S. competitiveness, particularly technology and clean energy.

Dear Readers: We recognize that politics often intersect with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Trump says group of wealthy investors ready to acquire TikTok
data.description:
U.S. President Donald Trump announced during a television interview on Sunday that a buyer has been identified for TikTok, consisting of what he described as "very wealthy people," though he said their identities will be revealed in approximately two weeks.

Trump shared the update during an appearance on Fox News Channel's "Sunday Morning Futures with Maria Bartiromo."

The social-video platform, which has been under mounting pressure to divest from Chinese ownership, faced a deadline earlier this year to secure a sale to a non-Chinese buyer or risk being banned in the United States.

data.title: Traders are said to shift hedges as Mideast tensions ease
data.description:
U.S. stocks reached fresh highs last week and oil prices pulled back as geopolitical tensions in the Middle East appeared to subside. Yet, beneath the surface of options markets, signs of caution persist as investors hedge against potential future shocks tied to global events or economic uncertainty, Bloomberg News reported Sunday.

In the short term, investor sentiment around equities is turning more optimistic. The cost of protective put options relative to calls has narrowed, reflecting reduced demand for downside protection. However, sentiment further out on the horizon remains cautious, with longer-term pricing dynamics largely unchanged. This includes elevated prices for longer-dated Cboe Volatility Index (VIX) futures, pointing to ongoing concerns about issues like tariffs and broader market stability.

The derivatives landscape hasn’t fully stabilized to where it was in February, said Rocky Fishman, founder of research firm Asym 500, in a report Friday. He said the VIX futures curve has steepened, with mid-range contracts around six months out staying at levels rarely seen in 2023 and 2024.

More bullish on near-term equities

Shrinking put premiums suggest confidence, but long-term positioning remains cautious.

Last week also brought signs of increased demand for VIX call options, contracts that typically profit from a spike in volatility. The demand was seen even as the Cboe VVIX Index, which measures volatility of volatility, slipped below 90, marking its lowest reading since July of last year.

In the oil market, volatility has receded following the Israel-Iran conflict, but caution remains. Brent crude’s (CO1:COM) implied volatility has returned to early June levels, with option pricing showing little directional bias. However, option premiums for oil swings remain elevated compared to equities, especially after the S&P 500’s (SP500) record-setting rally placed downward pressure on stock market volatility.

Traders eye oil-equity correlations

J.P. Morgan Chase (JPM) derivatives strategists, including Emma Wu, recently recommended hybrid trades linking oil and equities, Bloomberg News reported. They suggested that renewed geopolitical instability could push oil prices higher, while prolonged elevated interest rates could pressure stocks lower. Although oil and equities have shown increased positive correlation recently, the two tend to decouple during major geopolitical disruptions.

The recent rapid shifts in global risk have left many investors on edge. Data from ICE Futures Europe shows hedge funds and other large investors scaled back bullish bets on Brent crude at the fastest pace since April during the week ending June 24. This followed a period of building long positions, which had reached an 11-week high.

Meanwhile, trend-following Commodity Trading Advisors (CTAs) quickly reversed course in the European natural gas market. By last Tuesday, these funds shifted to an 18% net short position from 9% net long, according to Bridgeton Research Group data cited by Bloomberg News. Such algorithm-driven strategies often amplify price moves, complicating risk management for traders with exposure to physical commodities.

Crude oil calendar spreads attract heavy interest

One corner of the oil market seeing an unusual spike in activity is calendar spread options: contracts that allow traders to bet on the price difference between near-term and longer-dated oil futures. Open interest in these contracts has surged to record highs this month.

Traders appear to be wagering that short-term tightness in oil supply could give way to a surplus later in the year, as OPEC and other producers boost output while uncertainty around global demand persists. That curve structure, likened to a “hockey stick,” briefly disappeared during the height of the Israel-Iran conflict but has since reverted to its pre-crisis formation, Bloomberg News reported.

Geopolitical risk premium shrinks, but caution persists

Goldman Sachs analysts, including Yulia Zhestkova Grigsby and Daan Struyven, suggested in a recent note that the easing of geopolitical risk premiums reflects market expectations shaped by recent experience.

Major geopolitical flare-ups have occurred without severe disruptions to oil supplies, while both the U.S. and China appear motivated to avoid escalating tensions. They also pointed to expectations of significant inventory builds heading into the fall, further pressuring oil prices, Bloomberg News reported.

data.title: M&A activity rebounds amid market uncertainty, Trump’s trade policies
data.description:
Corporate dealmaking in the United States is off to its strongest first-half performance since 2022, signaling that appetite for mergers and acquisitions remains resilient in the face of market volatility, global conflicts and President Trump’s shifting tariff landscape, The Wall Street Journal reported on Sunday.

According to data from the London Stock Exchange Group cited by the newspaper, the total value of U.S. deals through June 25 has climbed roughly 10% compared to the same period last year, reaching its highest level in three years.

Deal activity stumbled early in the second quarter after Trump’s broad “Liberation Day” tariffs, introduced in April, rattled markets and temporarily dampened enthusiasm among dealmakers. However, activity has since regained momentum.

Several significant transactions have taken place in sectors less affected by Trump’s trade measures. Notable among them, Charter Communications (CHTR) agreed to acquire Cox Communications for nearly $22 billion, consolidating the broadband and cable market. In the tech sector, Salesforce (CRM) revived its approximately $8 billion acquisition of Informatica, underscoring the industry's push to strengthen AI capabilities.

Footwear brand Skechers also made headlines with a deal to take the company private, valued at more than $9 billion.

Despite the uptick in total deal value, the overall number of transactions has fallen 16% this year, a decline driven largely by fewer deals under $1 billion, which traditionally make up the bulk of market activity.

Advisers say some acquirers are holding back, waiting for more economic clarity before pursuing major transactions. Wall Street still anticipates a robust M&A cycle under Trump’s administration, though the timing may extend beyond initial expectations.

Private equity has continued to play an outsized role in the market. LSEG reports that private-equity-backed deals are up nearly 21% in value so far this year, although the number of transactions has dropped by 20%.

The conditions are aligning for stronger dealmaking in the second half of the year, particularly if inflation cools further and the Federal Reserve moves to lower interest rates, the Journal reported. Capital remains available, and private equity firms are under pressure to deliver returns to investors while putting their raised funds to work.

The market for new stock offerings is also showing signs of revival. Major U.S. indexes have rebounded following the initial turbulence from Trump’s tariffs.

Recent IPOs have added to the optimism. Virtual physical therapy provider Hinge Health (HNGE) saw its shares jump 17% in its market debut in late May. Two weeks later, Circle Internet Group, a cryptocurrency company, delivered one of the most talked-about listings in recent memory.

In another encouraging sign, companies that have gone public in the past six weeks have, on average, outperformed the broader market, suggesting investor appetite for new offerings is strengthening.

data.title: Notable healthcare headlines for the week: Novo Nordisk, Amgen and UnitedHealth in focus
data.description:
Wall Street’s major averages closed Friday's trading session higher, as both the S&P 500 (SP500) and Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) notched fresh all-time trading highs and record high closes.

The S&P 500 Health Care Index Sector (NYSEARCA:XLV) also gained over 1.50% during the week.

The top S&P 500 healthcare gainers and losers for the last week are as follows:

Top Gainers:

DexCom (DXCM) +7.92%

Edwards Lifesciences (EW) +5.46%

Boston Scientific (BSX) +5.19%

Intuitive Surgical (ISRG) +4.98%

Moderna (MRNA) +4.94%

Top Losers:

Amgen (AMGN) -4.19%

AbbVie (ABBV) -1.27%

Bristol-Myers Squibb (BMY) -1.18%

Quest Diagnostics (DGX) -1.01%

Biogen (BIIB) -0.87%

Here are some of the important healthcare stories from this week:

Novo Nordisk rolls out weight loss drug Wegovy in India, following Eli Lilly’s Mounjaro launch

Novo Nordisk (NVO) has launched its blockbuster weight-loss drug Wegovy (semaglutide) in India, shortly after rival Eli Lilly (LLY) introduced its popular weight loss/diabetes therapy Mounjaro in the country this March.

The Danish drugmaker will offer Wegovy as a once-weekly injection in five dosage strengths: 0.25 mg, 0.5 mg, 1 mg, 1.75 mg, and 2.4 mg.

Approved in India for chronic weight management and reducing the risk of major cardiovascular events, the drug will be priced at 4,366 rupees ($50.71) for the first three doses. Monthly treatment will cost 17,345 rupees, while higher doses are priced between 24,280 rupees and 26,015 rupees per month.

In other news of note, Novo Nordisk (NVO) said on Monday that it will cease its partnership with Hims & Hers Health (HIMS), ending the direct access to Wegovy, its weight loss drug, through NovoCare Pharmacy.

However, Hims & Hers Health CEO Andrew Dudum, in a Bloomberg interview, said that his telehealth platform will still make cheaper, compounded versions of the drug.

On the other hand, the Danish drugmaker partnered with WeightWatchers (OTC:WGHTQ) to sell its obesity drug Wegovy.

Amgen falls despite positive weight loss trial results

Amgen (AMGN) announced on Monday that its new weight loss treatment, MariTide, resulted in an average weight loss of approximately 20% over 52 weeks in a mid-stage trial involving individuals with obesity who do not have Type 2 diabetes.

The trial also indicated that participants with obesity and Type 2 diabetes experienced an average weight loss of around 17%, significantly higher than the 1.4% weight loss observed in the placebo group.

Amgen reported that the study showed no signs of reaching a weight loss plateau, suggesting that participants may continue to lose weight beyond the 52-week mark.

Despite these promising results, Amgen's stock price fell, as obesity therapies by Eli Lilly (LLY) and Novo Nordisk (NVO) have already demonstrated weight loss of at least 15% to 20% in overweight individuals and have established treatments available in the market.

UnitedHealth names new CEO for health services arm

UnitedHealth Group’s (UNH) new CEO, Stephen Hemsley, is shaking up the senior management of the company, appointing Patrick Conway, newly named CEO of the managed care giant’s Optum unit, as the head of its health services division, Optum Health.

Citing a company memo, Bloomberg News reported that Conway, who previously served as the CEO of Optum’s pharmacy benefits unit, Optum Rx, before getting promoted to lead the broader Optum unit last month, will also add the title of Optum Health CEO.

According to the memo, Amar Desai, who has led Optum Health since 2023, will become the vice chairman of the division and president of Optum Integrated Care, and he will continue to work with Hemsley and Conway.

Altimmune plunges after mid-stage trial data for MASH therapy

Altimmune (ALT) lost ~64% in the premarket on Thursday after the company announced a mixed readout from a mid-stage trial for pemvidutide, its experimental therapy for a liver condition known as metabolic dysfunction-associated steatohepatitis (MASH).

RFK Jr. says U.S. to end its funding of international vaccine alliance Gavi

HHS Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. on Wednesday told Gavi, an international alliance that provides vaccines to poor countries, that the U.S. is pulling its financial support.

RFK Jr., in a video address made at a Gavi event in Brussels, questioned the group's COVID-19 recommendations and has concerns surrounding the DTPw (diphtheria-tetanus-whole cell pertussis) vaccine.

data.title: Senate narrowly advances Trump’s tax and spending bill amid GOP divisions
data.description:
Senate Republicans narrowly cleared a key hurdle late Saturday to keep President Trump’s ambitious tax and spending package alive, though the razor-thin vote exposed the political headaches that lie ahead for GOP leaders, the Associated Press reported.

In a 51-49 vote, most Republicans backed the bill’s advancement, while Democrats uniformly opposed it. Senators Rand Paul (Republican, Kentucky) and Thom Tillis (Republican, North Carolina) broke with their party, voting no.

The bill, officially titled the "One Big Beautiful Bill Act," runs nearly 1,000 pages and remains subject to revisions. Senate leaders hope to pass the legislation by Monday, aligning with Trump’s July 4 target to finalize the package.

Though the bill has been months in the making, Saturday’s vote marked the first significant show of support and resistance. GOP leaders spent more than three hours negotiating to secure the votes needed to proceed, reflecting the delicate balancing act of aligning Trump’s priorities with the varying demands of Republicans across the ideological spectrum.

Senator Lisa Murkowski (Republican, Alaska), who has pushed to safeguard clean-energy tax breaks, was seen negotiating with Senate Finance Chair Mike Crapo (Republican, Idaho) before ultimately voting in favor.

Other holdouts included Sens. Rick Scott (Republican, Florida), Mike Lee (Republican, Utah), Ron Johnson (Republican, Wisconsin) and Cynthia Lummis (Republication, Wyoming), who sought steeper spending cuts.

Johnson, who initially opposed advancing the measure, switched his vote late in the evening after GOP leaders agreed to back a proposal to end new enrollments in the Medicaid expansion introduced under the Affordable Care Act. That idea remains controversial among moderate Republicans and faces an uncertain path.

More spending on border, defense

The legislation combines permanent tax cuts, new tax relief, significant boosts to border and defense spending and reductions in Medicaid and nutrition programs, hallmarks of Trump’s second-term agenda. Democrats have fiercely criticized the bill, calling it an assault on the social safety net paired with giveaways to high-income earners.

According to a Congressional Budget Office (CBO) analysis, the Senate version would cut the deficit by $508 billion, but only under the assumption that expiring tax cuts are extended indefinitely without adding costs.

When compared directly to the House proposal, the Senate bill would increase deficits by roughly $3.3 trillion, exceeding the $2.4 trillion projected for the House version. That growing deficit number has sparked concern among fiscal conservatives in the House, raising doubts about whether the bill can ultimately pass.

The CBO also projects that the latest Senate bill would leave nearly 12 million more Americans without health coverage by 2034.

Republicans have been working feverishly to consolidate support, deploying policy concessions and direct lobbying. Trump spent part of Saturday golfing with senators and holding meetings at the White House. He also used Truth Social to criticize Paul and Tillis after their votes.

Expanded SALT deductions

The latest Senate revisions include expanded state and local tax (SALT) deductions through 2029, providing temporary relief for residents of high-tax states. The bill also slows the phase-out of so-called provider taxes, which states use to leverage federal Medicaid funds.

On the energy front, the bill accelerates the rollback of tax credits for electric vehicles, solar and wind projects, a move favored by conservatives but opposed by some industry groups. A new excise tax on certain foreign-made components used in renewable projects has also been added.

Senator Josh Hawley (Republican, Missouri) voted in favor despite opposing changes to Medicaid funding formulas, noting that a $25 billion rural hospital fund in the bill would benefit his state in the short term. He pledged to fight future efforts to implement delayed Medicaid cuts.

Senator Susan Collins (Republican, Maine) supported moving the bill forward but remains undecided on the final legislation. Collins has called for curbing tax cuts for high earners and raised concerns about the Medicaid reductions.

Revisions to the SALT cap would set the deduction limit at $40,000 in 2025, with income phase-outs beginning at $500,000. These amounts would gradually increase through 2029 before the cap returns to $10,000. The bill also eliminates restrictions that previously prevented closely held businesses from circumventing the cap through state-level workarounds—a change welcomed by groups like dentists, manufacturers and accountants.

Small colleges successfully lobbied for an exemption from an endowment tax, which now applies only to institutions with more than 3,000 students.

The energy-related changes respond to complaints from conservatives and Trump that the GOP wasn’t moving fast enough to undo clean-energy subsidies enacted by Democrats in the 2022 Inflation Reduction Act. Under the bill, tax credits for electric vehicles would end by Sept. 30, while wind and solar projects must be operational by the end of 2027 to qualify for incentives. Hydrogen projects, however, receive extended timelines after lobbying from Senator Shelley Moore Capito (Republican, West Virginia). Tax credits for certain coal types used in steelmaking were also added.

Musk lashes out

Elon Musk, Tesla chief executive and former Trump adviser, took to social media to criticize the bill, saying it favors "industries of the past" at the expense of future-focused sectors like electric vehicles.

Senator Mike Lee (Republican, Utah) agreed to drop a controversial provision to sell off public lands after facing opposition from within his party.

The updated bill would gradually reduce the provider tax rate for states that expanded Medicaid under the Affordable Care Act, dropping from 6% to 3.5% beginning in 2028, one year later than previously proposed. States that didn’t expand Medicaid would see provider tax rates frozen. Nursing homes and intermediate care facilities are unaffected by the changes.

Alaska also secured targeted benefits after advocacy from Murkowski and Senator Dan Sullivan (Republican, Alaska). The bill increases tax deductions for Alaska Native whaling captains, adjusts federal Medicaid matching rates to account for Alaska and Hawaii’s higher living costs and provides flexibility to protect food assistance programs in Alaska.

Goldman Sachs, Trump, and Republican leadership remain determined to push the bill through both chambers before the July 4 deadline, though significant hurdles remain in uniting the party behind the sprawling legislation.

data.title: Prices for summer driving season lowest in four years despite Middle East turmoil
data.description:
U.S. gas prices at the pump are cheaper this summer, with regular gasoline averaging $3.19/gal nationally, $0.31 less than last year at this time, and analysts believe plentiful supply will keep prices down in the coming months even with turmoil in the Middle East.

The last time the cost for U.S. motorists was lower in late June was in 2021, when the COVID pandemic depressed demand for the fuel.

The price as tracked by AAA is only ~$0.03 higher than the national average a month ago, before Israel began its air strikes on Iran.

AAA expects travel for the Fourth of July holiday, which is on a Friday this year, will reach record highs: The group forecasts 72.2M Americans will travel domestically. including nearly 62M by auto, 2.2% more than in 2024.

Crude oil prices, which play a major role in the cost of gasoline, jumped sharply after Israel launched its strikes on Iran, but benchmark U.S. WTI prices plunged 11% this week to ~$65/bbl, lower than before the attacks.

The cost of crude oil previously was held down because of record-high U.S. output, and OPEC+ has been steadily increasing production; low energy prices so far this year have helped keep inflation in check and contributed to the economy's resilience.

Analysts believe the robust supply of crude oil, including some domestic production coming online, should stay ahead of demand and push prices even lower later this year.

U.S. gasoline prices should hold in the $3.00-$3.20/gal range for the summer, assuming the Israel-Iran conflict does not re-escalate, GasBuddy's Patrick De Haan said, adding that even if the conflict resumes, prices likely would not rise more than $0.15/gal.

ETFs: (NYSEARCA:USO), (SCO), (UCO), (USL), (DBO), (DRIP), (GUSH), (USOI), (NYSEARCA:UGA)

data.title: Senate Republicans release 940-page tax bill draft as Trump pushes for July 4 approval
data.description:
U.S. Senate Republicans on Saturday were planning to vote on President Donald Trump's sweeping tax cut and spending bill, a day after releasing a 940-page version of the "One Big Beautiful Bill Act" (OBBBA). Trump, for whom the bill is a top legislative goal, is pushing for its approval by Independence Day.

"We have before us today a once-in-a-generation opportunity to deliver legislation to create a safer, stronger, and more prosperous America. It’s time to get this legislation across the finish line," Senate Majority Leader John Thune (R-SD) said in remarks delivered from the Senate floor Saturday afternoon.

The Senate's version of the OBBBA would extend Trump's 2017 tax cuts - which are set to expire at the end of this year - and make them permanent. It also includes a $150B bump to military spending, while restricting Medicaid and making cuts to clean energy incentives.  

"By making the Trump tax cuts permanent, working families will avoid a four trillion-dollar tax increase. Our bill provides full funding to secure the border in perpetuity and injects a much-needed $150B into our military to keep our nation safe. In addition, the bill raises the debt ceiling so that we do not default and crash the economy," Senator Lindsey Graham (R-SC) said in Friday's Senate statement. 

The Committee for a Responsible Federal Budget, a nonpartisan public policy organization, on Saturday said the Senate's version of the OBBBA would likely add about $4T to the national debt, including interest. 

See the full Senate text of the OBBBA here.

President Trump held an event on Friday celebrating the legislation, ahead of which he had said: "the House of Representatives must be ready to send it to my desk before July 4th — We can get it done." The U.S. House passed its own version of the bill in May, and would also need to sign off on the Senate version before it heads to Trump. 

For Wall Street, the push to pass the OBBBA has been mostly overshadowed by geopolitical conflicts and trade developments. Stocks hit record highs this week for the first time since February. 

Here are some exchange-traded funds that track the benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) index: (NYSEARCA:SPY), (NYSEARCA:VOO), (NYSEARCA:IVV), (NYSEARCA:RSP), (NYSEARCA:SSO), (NYSEARCA:UPRO), (NYSEARCA:SH), (NYSEARCA:SDS), and (NYSEARCA:SPXU). 

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Regional bank stocks could outperform if these three catalysts land: BofA
data.description:
U.S. regional banking stocks have lagged behind their larger peers and the broader equity market, though a handful of policy and macroeconomic catalysts could change that, according to Bank of America Securities.

Since the start of the year, regional lenders (KRX) slid 2.9% as of Friday's close, trailing large-cap peers' (BKX) 9.1% climb. That compares with the S&P 500 index's 5% rise.

Meanwhile, analyst Ebrahim Poonawala laid out several catalysts that could pave the way for regionals to gain ground. He specifically pointed to certain tailwinds that would boost confidence in the Federal Reserve cutting interest rates and engineering a soft landing for the U.S. economy.

The following regionals offer a particularly attractive risk-reward profile, he pointed out: KeyCorp (NYSE:KEY), Truist (NYSE:TFC), M&T bank (NYSE:MTB), Synovus Financial (NYSE:SNV), East West Bancorp (NASDAQ:EWBC), Webster Financial (NYSE:WBS), UMB Financial (NASDAQ:UMBF), FNB Corp. (NYSE:FNB) and Western Alliance Bancorporation (NYSE:WAL).

"While regionals have trailed, we see improving macro-outlook and a pick-up in bank M&A as serving as catalysts to drive flows into the group," Poonawala wrote in a note to clients.

The first catalyst centers on the Federal Reserve's annual stress test results, which showed that all 22 banks tested above the minimum common equity tier 1 (CET1) capital requirement during a hypothetical recession. The analyst sees a potential rotation into regionals on the back of the results, as investors priced in a reduction in CET1 capital requirements.

Elsewhere, the June jobs report, set to land on July 3, serves as another trigger. "Any material cracks in the job market could reignite credit quality concerns, barely a passing thought for most investors at the moment," Poonawala wrote, pointing to gradual gains in initial and continuing jobless claims as of recent.

The final trigger focuses on the 90-day tariff pause, which ends on July 9 and "has the potential to serve as a positive or negative catalyst," the note said. "UK-style trade agreements with key trading partners could provide assurance that “peak chaos” due to tariffs is in the rearview mirror." That, in turn, could be a boon for lending growth and middle market investment banking activity.

Earlier this week, Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick said that the U.S. and China have finalized a trade truce reached last month, adding that the Trump administration plans to reach deals with 10 other major trading partners.

Seeking Alpha's Quant system gives Popular (BPOP) the highest rank among publicly traded regional banks, followed by Ponce Financial Group (PDLB), Metropolitan Bank Holding (MCB) and Capitol Federal Financial (CFFN).

SA contributor MTS Insights remains relatively cautious about the outlook for regional banks, highlighting fiscal risks and concerns over U.S. debt and deficits. "There are key macro tailwinds in an improving growth outlook and a disinflationary trend in the last few months of price data. However, the focus on the deteriorating fiscal situation in the US overshadows them, and because it is center stage in investors’ minds, it causes me to pause."

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.
